title: Partition Shapes into Equal Parts
essential_question: How can you partition a shape into equal parts and describe each part?

pretest.question: A circle is divided into 2 equal parts. Each part is a __

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: 2 equal parts has a special name.

feedback.correct: Yes! 2 equal parts means each part is a half.
feedback.incorrect: 2 equal parts = halves. Each part is one half (1/2).

pretest.feedback_correct: Yes! 2 equal parts means each part is a half.

pretest.question: A rectangle is partitioned into 4 equal parts. What fraction is one part? (Write as a fraction like 1/4)

4/4.note: That is the whole, not one part

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: 4 equal parts: each is 1 part out of 4.

feedback.correct: Right! Each part is 1/4 of the rectangle.
feedback.incorrect: Each of the 4 equal parts is 1/4 of the whole.

pretest.feedback_correct: Right! Each part is 1/4 of the rectangle.

pretest.question: Which is true about equal parts?
choices: A) Equal parts can be different sizes
B) Equal parts must be the same size
C) Only the largest part counts
D) Equal parts must be different shapes

A.note: Equal parts must be the SAME size

D.note: Equal parts have the same size

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: What does 'equal' mean?

feedback.correct: Yes! Equal parts must all be the same size.
feedback.incorrect: Equal parts must be the SAME size. If parts are different sizes, they are NOT equal.

pretest.feedback_correct: Yes! Equal parts must all be the same size.

pretest.question: A pizza is cut into 6 equal slices. Each slice is a ___. (Write the name like 'fourth' or 'sixth')

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: 6 equal parts has a name that ends in 'th'.

feedback.correct: Right! Each slice is a sixth (1/6).
feedback.incorrect: 6 equal parts are called sixths. Each slice is 1/6 of the pizza.

pretest.feedback_correct: Right! Each slice is a sixth (1/6).

pretest.question: A rectangle is cut into 4 parts. Two of the parts are big, and two are small. Are the parts EQUAL?
choices: A) Yes, because there are 4 parts
B) Yes, because they are all parts of the same shape
C) No — the parts are different sizes
D) Yes, because rectangles always have equal parts

A.note: Number of parts doesn't make them equal

B.note: Equal parts must be the same size

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: What does 'equal' mean?

feedback.correct: Right! Different sizes means the parts are NOT equal.
feedback.incorrect: The parts must be the SAME size to be equal. Different sizes = not equal.

pretest.feedback_correct: Right! Different sizes means the parts are NOT equal.

learn.title: What Is Partitioning?
learn.content: To PARTITION a shape is to divide it into parts. When the parts are all the same size, we call them EQUAL PARTS. Examples: cutting a pizza into 4 equal slices, cutting a chocolate bar into 6 equal pieces. Each piece is a part of the WHOLE.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Equal Parts Must Be the Same Size
learn.content: EQUAL parts means same SIZE. If you cut a pizza so one slice is bigger and another is smaller, those are NOT equal parts. To make equal parts, every piece must be exactly the same size — like 4 perfectly matching pizza slices.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Halves — 2 Equal Parts
learn.content: When a shape is partitioned into 2 EQUAL parts, each part is a HALF. The fraction for one half is 1/2 (one out of two). Two halves together make a whole. Example: a circle cut down the middle makes 2 halves.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Thirds and Fourths
learn.content: THIRDS = 3 equal parts. Each is 1/3. THIRDS + THIRDS + THIRDS = WHOLE. FOURTHS = 4 equal parts. Each is 1/4. Four fourths make a whole. Example: a rectangle cut into 4 equal rows shows fourths.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Sixths and Eighths
learn.content: SIXTHS = 6 equal parts. Each is 1/6. EIGHTHS = 8 equal parts. Each is 1/8. The MORE equal parts a shape is divided into, the SMALLER each part becomes. A shape cut into 8 parts has smaller pieces than the same shape cut into 4 parts.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Different Ways to Partition the Same Shape
learn.content: You can partition a square into 4 equal parts in different ways: (1) 4 equal rows, (2) 4 equal columns, (3) 2 rows and 2 columns. All three give 4 equal parts. The shape of each part may LOOK different (rectangle vs square), but each part still equals 1/4 of the whole.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: More Parts → Smaller Pieces
learn.content: When the SAME WHOLE is partitioned into more parts, each part is SMALLER. Order from biggest to smallest piece for the same whole: 1/2 > 1/3 > 1/4 > 1/6 > 1/8. The denominator (bottom number) tells you how many equal parts — bigger denominator means smaller piece.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Lesson Summary
learn.content: Today you learned: (1) PARTITION = divide a shape into parts. (2) Equal parts must be the SAME SIZE. (3) Names: 2=halves, 3=thirds, 4=fourths, 6=sixths, 8=eighths. (4) Each part is named by the fraction 1/n where n is the total number of equal parts. (5) More parts = smaller pieces. (6) The same shape can be partitioned in different ways.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

try.question: A rectangle is partitioned into 8 equal parts. What fraction is one part? (Write as 1/n)
solution_steps: Step 2: Each part is 1 out of 8.

8/8.note: That is the whole, not one part

feedback.correct: Right! Each of the 8 equal parts is 1/8 of the whole.
feedback.incorrect: Each part of an 8-part partition is 1/8 of the whole.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes

feedback.correct: Right! 3 equal parts means each part is a third.
feedback.incorrect: 3 equal parts are called thirds.

hints: What is the name for 3 equal parts?

try.question: Which figure shows EQUAL parts?
choices: A) A rectangle cut into 4 strips of different widths
B) A pizza with 2 large slices and 4 tiny slices
C) A circle cut into 4 same-size wedges
D) A square with 1 big half and 1 small half
solution_steps: Step 1: Equal parts must be the same size.
Step 2: 4 same-size wedges in a circle = equal parts.
Step 3: The other choices have parts of different sizes.

D.note: Halves must be the SAME size

feedback.correct: Right! Same-size wedges = equal parts.
feedback.incorrect: Equal parts must all be the same size. Only choice C shows that.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: Equal means SAME size.

try.question: If each part of a rectangle is 1/6, how many equal parts is the rectangle divided into?

1.note: 1 is the numerator. Denominator (6) is the count of parts

feedback.correct: Right! 1/6 means 6 equal parts total.
feedback.incorrect: The denominator (6) tells you the number of equal parts. So 6.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: What does the bottom number of 1/6 tell you?

try.question: Which is BIGGER: 1/4 of a square or 1/8 of the same square?
solution_steps: Step 4: 1/4 > 1/8 (4 parts is fewer, so each part is bigger).

B.note: More parts means smaller pieces; 1/8 is smaller

C.note: Different number of parts → different sizes

feedback.correct: Right! 1/4 > 1/8 because fewer parts means each part is bigger.
feedback.incorrect: 1/4 is bigger. With fewer equal parts (4 vs 8), each part is bigger.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015
verification.original_ref: Same whole: more parts → smaller pieces.

practice_r3.question: Anna divides a square into 8 equal parts. Lily divides the same-size square into 4 equal parts. How many of Anna's parts equal ONE of Lily's parts?
solution_steps: Step 3: 1/4 = 2/8, so 2 of Anna's parts equal 1 of Lily's.

feedback.correct: Right! 1/4 = 2/8. 2 of Anna's eighths equal 1 of Lily's fourths.
feedback.incorrect: 1/4 = 2/8. So 2 of Anna's eighths equal 1 of Lily's fourths.

practice_r3.three_read_prompt: Read 1: How many parts did Anna and Lily make? Read 2: What is each part as a fraction? Read 3: How many of Anna's fit in 1 of Lily's?

verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

practice_r3.feedback_correct: Right! 1/4 = 2/8. 2 of Anna's eighths equal 1 of Lily's fourths.

feedback.correct: Right! Tom shaded 2 out of 6 equal parts, which is 2/6.
feedback.incorrect: 2 shaded parts out of 6 = 2/6.

practice_r3.three_read_prompt: Read 1: How many equal parts? Read 2: How many shaded? Read 3: What fraction?

practice_r3.question: Ben divides a chocolate bar into 8 equal pieces. He gives Anna 1 piece and Sara 3 pieces. What fraction of the bar is LEFT?

feedback.correct: Right! 8 pieces − 4 given = 4 left. 4/8 of the bar is left.
feedback.incorrect: Ben started with 8 pieces and gave away 1+3=4. Left = 8-4 = 4. So 4/8.

practice_r3.three_read_prompt: Read 1: How many pieces total? Read 2: How many given away? Read 3: How many left?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.13 Lesson — U13_shapes/L5_partition_shapes

hints: Add given pieces, then subtract from 8.
practice_r3.feedback_correct: Right! 8 pieces − 4 given = 4 left. 4/8 of the bar is left.
